title: Functions & Arguments
subtitle: def replaces method signatures — and adds superpowers

sections.java: // Type-first signature, return type required public double discount(double price, double pct) { return price * (1 - pct); } // Overloading for optional args public double discount(double price) { return discount(price, 0.1); }
sections.python: # def, name-first, hints after the colon def discount(price: float, pct: float = 0.1) -> float: return price * (1 - pct) # Default values replace overloading entirely discount(100) # uses pct=0.1 discount(100, 0.2) # override

sections.title: Keyword Arguments Kill Overloading
sections.content: Python has no method overloading — and rarely needs it. Default parameter values plus keyword arguments cover the cases. Call discount(price=100, pct=0.2) by name for self-documenting calls. One def replaces a whole family of Java overloads.

sections.title: The #1 Python Bug: Mutable Default Arguments
sections.content: def add(item, bucket=[]): is a trap. The default [] is created once at definition time and shared across all calls — so it accumulates across invocations. Always use None as the sentinel: def add(item, bucket=None): bucket = bucket or []. This bites every Java dev exactly once.

sections.java: // Varargs public int sum(int... nums) { int total = 0; for (int n : nums) total += n; return total; } // No clean keyword-args equivalent
sections.python: # *args = varargs, **kwargs = keyword map def sum_all(*nums: int) -> int: return sum(nums) def configure(**opts: str) -> None: for key, val in opts.items(): print(f"{key}={val}") configure(host="db", port="5432")

sections.title: Functions Are First-Class Objects
sections.content: Unlike Java (where you need a Function/Supplier interface or method reference), Python functions are plain objects. Assign them, pass them, return them: handlers = {"json": parse_json, "csv": parse_csv} then handlers[fmt](data). No functional interface ceremony.

sections.java: // Lambda needs a functional interface Function<Integer, Integer> sq = x -> x * x; list.stream() .filter(x -> x > 0) .map(sq) .collect(Collectors.toList());
sections.python: # lambda is a bare expression — no interface sq = lambda x: x * x # But comprehensions usually read better: result = [x * x for x in nums if x > 0] # lambdas shine as sort keys: users.sort(key=lambda u: u.age)

sections.title: Lambdas Are One Expression Only
sections.content: Python lambda can't contain statements — no if/return blocks, no multiple lines. If your lambda wants a body, just use a named def (they're cheap and can be nested). Most Java Stream pipelines become Python comprehensions (Module 08), not lambda chains.

tabs.label: Closures
tabs.content:
# A closure remembers the enclosing scope
def multiplier(factor: int):
    def multiply(n: int) -> int:
        return n * factor   # factor is captured
    return multiply

triple = multiplier(3)
triple(10)   # 30

Java lambdas may only capture effectively-final variables. Python closures can even reassign a captured name with nonlocal — a true closure over mutable state.

tabs.label: functools
tabs.content:
from functools import reduce, partial, lru_cache

# reduce == Stream.reduce
total = reduce(lambda a, b: a + b, nums, 0)

# partial pre-binds arguments (currying)
local_db = partial(connect, "localhost")
local_db(5432)   # connect("localhost", 5432)

@lru_cache   # automatic memoization
def fib(n): return n if n < 2 else fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)

tabs.label: operator & map
tabs.content:
from operator import attrgetter, itemgetter

# Named functions for sort keys
users.sort(key=attrgetter("age"))   # ~ User::getAge
rows.sort(key=itemgetter(0))       # by column 0

# map/filter exist but are lazy iterators
names = list(map(str.upper, raw))
# Prefer a comprehension when you'd write a lambda:
names = [r.upper() for r in raw]

tabs.label: Full pipeline
tabs.content:
from functools import reduce

# Compose filter -> map -> reduce: no loops, no mutation
paid    = filter(lambda o: o["paid"], orders)
amounts = map(lambda o: o["amount"], paid)
total   = reduce(lambda a, b: a + b, amounts, 0.0)

# Same result, idiomatic Python (comprehension):
total = sum(o["amount"] for o in orders if o["paid"])

A complete functional pipeline built from pure functions and lazy map/filter, folded with reduce — the direct analog of Java's orders.stream().filter(...).mapToDouble(...).sum().

sections.question: Why is `def append_to(item, target=[]):` considered a bug?
options: Lists can't be default arguments in Python
The default list is created once and shared across all calls, accumulating values
It's a syntax error without a type hint
sections.explanation: Default argument values are evaluated once, when the function is defined — not on each call. So a mutable default like [] is the same object every call and accumulates state. The fix: default to None and create a fresh list inside the body.
